---
title: "Random Forest"
author: "Sananthaa J S"
date: "2026-03-29"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
library(randomForest)  
library(pROC)          
library(rms)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
df <- read.csv("D:/Giloma Grading Mutation Prediction/Glioma-grading-clinical-mutation-prediction/DATA/glioma+grading+clinical+and+mutation+features+dataset/TCGA_InfoWithGrade.csv")

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    Grade_f = factor(Grade, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("LGG", "GBM"))
  )

# identical split to Stages 2 and 3
set.seed(123)
train_idx <- c(
  sample(which(df$Grade == 0), size = round(0.7 * sum(df$Grade == 0))),
  sample(which(df$Grade == 1), size = round(0.7 * sum(df$Grade == 1)))
)
train <- df[train_idx, ]
test  <- df[-train_idx, ]

cat("Train:", nrow(train), "| Test:", nrow(test), "\n")
```

#predict Grade_f (factor) so randomForest returns class probabilities.

```{r }
set.seed(123)
model_rf <- randomForest(
  Grade_f ~ Age_at_diagnosis + Gender + Race +
            IDH1 + TP53 + ATRX + PTEN + EGFR + CIC + MUC16 + PIK3CA +
            NF1 + PIK3R1 + FUBP1 + RB1 + NOTCH1 + BCOR + CSMD3 +
            SMARCA4 + GRIN2A + IDH2 + FAT4 + PDGFRA,
  data       = train,
  ntree      = 500,
  importance = TRUE
)

print(model_rf)
```

```{r }
# extract and sort
imp_df <- data.frame(
  Gene       = rownames(importance(model_rf)),
  Importance = importance(model_rf)[, "MeanDecreaseGini"]
) %>%
  arrange(desc(Importance))

print(imp_df)

# plot
ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = Importance,
                   y = reorder(Gene, Importance))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3B8BD4") +
  labs(title    = "Variable importance — Random Forest",
       subtitle = "MeanDecreaseGini — higher = more important",
       x = "Mean decrease in Gini", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()
```


```{r }
pred_rf_prob  <- predict(model_rf, newdata = test, type = "prob")[, "GBM"]
pred_rf_class <- predict(model_rf, newdata = test, type = "class")

# confusion matrix
conf_rf <- table(Predicted = pred_rf_class, Actual = test$Grade_f)
print(conf_rf)

TP <- conf_rf["GBM", "GBM"]
TN <- conf_rf["LGG", "LGG"]
FP <- conf_rf["GBM", "LGG"]
FN <- conf_rf["LGG", "GBM"]

acc_rf  <- (TP + TN) / sum(conf_rf)
sens_rf <- TP / (TP + FN)
spec_rf <- TN / (TN + FP)

cat("Accuracy:   ", round(acc_rf,  3), "\n")
cat("Sensitivity:", round(sens_rf, 3), "\n")
cat("Specificity:", round(spec_rf, 3), "\n")
```

```{r }
auc_rf   <- auc(roc(test$Grade, pred_rf_prob))
brier_rf <- mean((pred_rf_prob - test$Grade)^2)

cat("AUC:        ", round(auc_rf,   3), "\n")
cat("Brier score:", round(brier_rf, 4), "\n")
```

```{r }
roc_rf <- roc(test$Grade, pred_rf_prob)

plot(roc_rf,
     col  = "#1D9E75",
     lwd  = 2,
     main = paste0("ROC curve — Random Forest (AUC = ",
                   round(auc_rf, 3), ")"))
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "grey60")
```

```{r }
val.prob(pred_rf_prob, test$Grade)
title("Calibration — Random Forest (uncalibrated)", line = 0.5)
```

```{r }
# fit a logistic regression on RF probabilities using training set
train_rf_prob <- predict(model_rf, newdata = train, type = "prob")[, "GBM"]

platt_model <- glm(Grade ~ train_rf_prob,
                   data   = data.frame(Grade        = train$Grade,
                                       train_rf_prob = train_rf_prob),
                   family = binomial)

# apply to test set
pred_rf_calibrated <- predict(platt_model,
                               newdata = data.frame(train_rf_prob = pred_rf_prob),
                               type    = "response")

# recalculate Brier after calibration
brier_rf_cal <- mean((pred_rf_calibrated - test$Grade)^2)
auc_rf_cal   <- auc(roc(test$Grade, pred_rf_calibrated))

cat("After Platt scaling:\n")
cat("AUC:        ", round(auc_rf_cal,   3), "\n")
cat("Brier score:", round(brier_rf_cal, 4), "\n")

# calibration after Platt scaling
val.prob(pred_rf_calibrated, test$Grade)
title("Calibration — Random Forest (Platt scaled)", line = 0.5)
```

```{r }
# calibration slopes — fill in from val.prob plots above
slope_rf       <- 0.864   # from uncalibrated RF calibration plot
slope_rf_platt <- 0.741  # from Platt-scaled calibration plot

comparison <- tibble(
  Model       = c("Logistic regression", "Lasso", "Ridge",
                  "Random Forest", "RF + Platt scaling"),
  AUC         = c(0.929, 0.914, 0.926,
                  round(auc_rf, 3), round(auc_rf_cal, 3)),
  Brier_score = c(0.0948, 0.1002, 0.0983,
                  round(brier_rf, 4), round(brier_rf_cal, 4)),
  Cal_slope   = c(0.962, 1.056, 1.387,
                  slope_rf, slope_rf_platt),
  Sensitivity = c(0.915, 0.925, 0.934,
                  round(sens_rf, 3), NA),
  Specificity = c(0.877, 0.849, 0.870,
                  round(spec_rf, 3), NA)
)

print(comparison)
```
NOTES

RF makes no linearity assumptions — can capture IDH1 × age interactions
Variable importance plot: does RF agree with logistic regression on which predictors matter most?
RF typically has high AUC but poor calibration — the slope will likely be far from 1
Platt scaling is a simple fix — one logistic regression on top of RF probabilities — show before vs after calibration slope
Key question: does RF beat AUC=0.929 from plain logistic regression?
If not, the simpler model wins
